topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay crumpled on the kitchen counter, a stark white document mocking me. My hands, usually steady, trembled as I stared at the genetic markers. They were undeniable. The results confirmed what my gut had been screaming for months: I wasn't just *a* child; I was *their* child. I didn't feel sadness, not exactly. Instead, a hot, tight knot formed in my chest, and I shoved the paper into the recycling bin with unnecessary force. "So what?" I muttered to the empty room, the sound sharp and brittle. I walked out onto the balcony, the city noise a dull roar beneath my pounding pulse. I didn't look back at the house, didn't acknowledge the life I was suddenly being handed. I just stood there, breathing in the cold air, feeling utterly unmoored and determined to find my own footing, however shaky the ground felt beneath my feet.

[story 2]

Dr. Evans’s words echoed in the sterile examination room, but the real revelation hit me later, alone in my cramped apartment, when I ran the test myself. The DNA report confirmed the truth: I was adopted. A slow burn started in my stomach, a simmering resentment that quickly boiled over into sharp, defensive anger. I picked up my phone, the screen illuminating my face, and started typing a message to the agency. My fingers flew across the keys, each word laced with a challenging edge. *“I don’t need your paperwork. I don’t need your narrative.”* I slammed the phone down onto the desk, the sound echoing the sudden, fierce refusal to accept the script they had written for me. I stood up, pacing the small space, kicking at an old box. I needed to dismantle this carefully constructed reality piece by piece, starting with the assumption that I was simply a replacement.

[story 3]

The silence in the living room was deafening after I read the report. It wasn't a shock that paralyzed me; it was a cold, hard clarity. I looked at my reflection in the darkened television screen—a stranger wearing my face. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped my lips, a sound more like a scoff than a sigh. I didn't cry. Instead, I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass. The world outside seemed too bright, too full of expectations I hadn't asked for. "Fine," I whispered to the glass, my voice low and steady, devoid of any vulnerability. I turned away from the view, my shoulders straightening beneath my worn sweater. Whatever history they held, it didn't dictate my next move. I felt a surge of pure, unyielding resolve, a stubborn refusal to be defined by bloodlines or paperwork.
